title: Constraint-Enhanced Physical Search through Correlation Matching
abstract: Physical systems do not merely add noise to search processes; they impose constraints that generate structured correlations. We propose a principle of constraint-enhanced physical search in which temporal correlations in exploration are matched to constraint-induced spatial correlations in the update dynamics. Using a minimal tug-of-war bandit model (TOW), we show that a conservation law converts local observations into differential evidence across alternatives, while a temporally correlated drive controls the order of exploration. Search efficiency is improved not by stronger randomness or by maximal anti-correlation, but by matching the temporal correlation to the physical update scale that converts feedback into evidence. A scaling estimate identifies the update-noise-to-contrast ratio as the leading parameter that limits how strongly temporal anti-correlation can be used. The results suggest a general organizing principle for physical search: constraints and fluctuations can generate structured spatiotemporal correlations, and efficient exploration emerges when these correlations are matched to the update dynamics.
summary: This paper proposes a unifying principle for efficient physical search by showing that optimal exploration requires matching the time scale of random fluctuations to the spatial scale imposed by physical constraints. Using a minimal Tug-of-War bandit model, the authors demonstrate that the synergy between temporal anti-correlation and conservation-induced updates drives efficiency, limited by the update-noise-to-contrast ratio.
main_problem: To develop a general principle for efficient physical search by matching temporal correlations in exploration to constraint-induced spatial correlations in update dynamics.
main_result: Efficient search emerges when the temporal correlation structure is tuned to match the physical update scale, quantified by a scaling estimate involving the update-noise-to-contrast ratio.
method: The authors analyze the problem using a minimal Tug-of-War (TOW) bandit model, deriving efficiency gains and interaction diagnostics ($\Lambda$) by comparing correlated versus uncorrelated sampling.
model_or_system: The core model is the minimal Tug-of-War (TOW) bandit, which compares two alternatives (A and B) and features a conservation-induced, anti-correlated update mechanism. The dynamics are governed by a correlated exploratory drive ($\sigma_t$) and the TOW update rule.
key_observables: Efficiency gain ($E_{\chi\kappa}$), Synthesis Coefficient ($\Lambda$), and the optimal temporal correlation ($\lambda^*$).
important_parameters: The update-noise-to-contrast ratio ($N$), the temporal non-redundancy proxy ($\chi$), and the conservation strength ($\kappa$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a stationary environment and treats the TOW dynamics as a controllable reduced system, focusing on the interaction between the two types of correlations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the TOW model cycle, show efficiency gain vs. correlation strength ($\lambda$), and use the Synthesis coefficient ($\Lambda$) to diagnose constructive interaction between temporal and spatial correlations.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the physical search problem, models it using the TOW bandit, develops diagnostics ($\Lambda$) to quantify correlation synergy, derives scaling laws based on balancing exploration gain against update noise cost, and concludes with a general principle for efficient physical exploration.
why_interesting: The concept of matching temporal fluctuations to physical constraints for optimal information gathering has parallels in understanding how quantum measurements or environmental coupling can guide quantum computation or state preparation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Minimal TOW bandit model. A two-armed bandit trial consists of choosing an arm at ∈{A, B}, observing a binary reward Rt ∈{0, 1}, and updating the internal TOW values QA and QB. For κ = 1, the update gives exact TOW conservation QA + QB = const. The differential evidence Zt = QA −QB is compared with a zero choice boundary after modulation by the temporally correlated drive σst. Reward feedback defines the update increment dt, and the TOW rule updates the chosen arm and the competing arm in opposite directions. Thus a local reward observation is converted into an option-wise anti-correlated update of the internal state.

Figure 2: Efficiency gain and synthesis diagnostic for temporal anti-correlation combined with TOW conservation. (a) Combined efficiency Eχκ = R00/Rχκ as a function of the temporal correlation parameter λ for the representative setting PA = 0.7, PB = 0.5, σ = 1.3, η = 0.5, and κ = 1. Values above unity indicate reduced regret relative to the uncorrelated one- sided baseline. The high-statistics data show a robust efficiency gain over the tested range of negative temporal correlations. Error bars show propagated standard errors obtained from the standard errors of the regret estimates. (b) Synthesis coefficient Λ, which isolates the non-additive interaction between temporal anti-correlation...

Figure 3: Internal mechanism behind the optimum. (a) Temporal anti-correlation improves early exploration balance during the phase in which the drive can still affect the decision boundary. (b) The variance of the differential update obeys the expected TOW scaling Var(∆Z) ∝η2, confirming the update-noise cost in Eq. (17). (c) Increasing η also increases premature or wrong locking, showing why the transduction from exploration to evidence formation has a finite window rather than growing monotonically with update strength.

Figure 4: Parameter sweeps supporting the scaling interpretation. (a) Changing the reward contrast ∆P at fixed ω0 = 1.5 reshapes the efficiency landscape over temporal correlations. (b) Changing the non-reward penalty ω0 at fixed ∆P = 0.20 also modifies the efficiency landscape. (c,d) Extracted optima |λ∗| plotted against the dimensionless update-noise ratio N = η(1 + κ)(1 + ω0)/∆P. Here N is the update-noise-to-reward-contrast ratio: large N means that the physical update scale is large relative to the reward difference that must be resolved.
